text:
National Sock Amnesty Day falls on the third Tuesday of every February, right when winter has overstayed its welcome and everyone is at their most irritable. The premise is simple: for one glorious day, you are legally and morally permitted to throw out every unmatched sock in your home without guilt, grief, or ceremony.

The celebration begins in the morning with the Drawer Reckoning, during which participants dump their entire sock collection onto the bed and confront the chaos honestly. Mismatched pairs that have been given the benefit of the doubt for months — years, in some households — are finally released from their purgatory. There is no more "maybe the other one will turn up." It will not turn up. It never turns up.

Traditionalists mark the occasion by holding a brief reading over the sock pile, something along the lines of "you tried," before committing the orphans to the trash. More theatrical celebrants host Sock Tribunals, where each unmatched sock is held up and given thirty seconds of defense before the household votes on its fate.

Children participate by decorating the surviving matched pairs with fabric markers, which accomplishes nothing practical but gives the holiday a festive craft component that parents can post online.

The evening meal is, by custom, whatever you want, because you just cleaned out your sock drawer and you deserve it.

Greeting cards exist but are deeply unnecessary. The standard salutation is simply "May your pairs stay together," which sounds sentimental until you remember you are talking about socks.
